title: The Bucolics and Eclogues
summaries: "The Bucolics and Eclogues" by Virgil is a collection of ten poems written between roughly 44 and 38 BC. Taking inspiration from Greek bucolic poetry, Virgil crafts dialogues and monologues featuring herdsmen in rural settings, weaving together themes of love, loss, and political upheaval during Rome's revolutionary period. Through singing contests, confiscated lands, and passionate declarations, these poems blend visionary politics with eroticism, creating a work that brought Virgil celebrity in his lifetime and established a new Roman literary tradition.
